architecture_summary: CoCa (Contrastive Captioner) is a minimalist image-text encoder-decoder foundation model that jointly pretrains with contrastive loss and captioning loss. It decouples the decoder into unimodal layers without cross-attention and multimodal layers with cross-attention to the image encoder, and uses attentional pooling for task-specific visual representations.

novel_contributions: A minimalist Contrastive Captioner (CoCa) that unifies single-encoder, dual-encoder, and encoder-decoder paradigms in one model
A decoupled decoder design that splits unimodal and multimodal layers by omitting cross-attention in the first half of decoder layers
Task-specific attentional pooling to produce different-length visual representations for contrastive and generative objectives
Single-stage pretraining from scratch on both web-scale alt-text data and annotated images by treating all labels as text
State-of-the-art performance across visual recognition, crossmodal retrieval, multimodal understanding, and image captioning with a single checkpoint

key_claims.claim: CoCa is a minimalist image-text foundation model that jointly pretrains with contrastive loss and captioning loss, subsuming CLIP-like and SimVLM-like capabilities.
CoCa omits cross-attention in the first half of decoder layers to encode unimodal text representations and uses the remaining layers for multimodal image-text representations.
CoCa is pretrained end-to-end and from scratch on both web-scale alt-text data and annotated images by treating all labels simply as text.
CoCa obtains 86.3% zero-shot top-1 accuracy, 90.6% with a frozen encoder, and 91.0% with a finetuned encoder on ImageNet.
CoCa sets new state-of-the-art results on a broad range of downstream tasks spanning visual recognition, crossmodal retrieval, multimodal understanding, and image captioning.
The model uses a single forward propagation to compute both contrastive and captioning losses with minimal overhead compared to standard encoder-decoder models.
CoCa is pretrained on JFT-3B and ALIGN in a single stage, with 500k steps and a batch size of 65,536 image-text pairs.
